messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

[101181988] [SORT4CIRC] Page 5 on 42 Confidential Abbreviation Definition AI Artificial Intelligence API Application Programming Interface BM Business Model CCRI Circular Cities and Regions Initiative CE Circular Economy DfD Design for Disassembly DPP Digital Product Passport EBSI European Blockchain Services Infrastructure EPR Extended Producer Responsibility ERP Enterprise Resource Planning FAT Factory Acceptance Tests FMEA Failure Mode and Effects Analysis FTIR Fourier Transform Infrared IoT Internet of Things LCA Life Cycle Assessment LCC Life Cycle Cost LCI Life Cycle Inventory LIBS Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy ML Machine Learning NIR Near InfraRed Abbreviation Definition OWL Web Ontology Language PCT Post-Consumer Textile PET Polyethylene terephthalate PET/CO Polyethylene terephthalate/Cotton PET/EA Polyethylene terephthalate/Elastane PET/PA Polyethylene terephthalate/Polyamide PoC Proof of Concept PoS Proof of Stake PSFS Pre-Sorting and facilitating Fine Sorting PSSR Pre-Sorting and Sorting for Recycling RDF Resource Description Framework RFID Radio Frequency IDentification ROI Return on Investment SC Supply Chain SKU Stock Keeping Unit SWIR/MWIR Short/Mid-Wave InfraRed TEA Technico-Economic Assessment TEF Technical & Economic Feasibility TTRFID Textile customised Tokenised RFID TVC Textile Value Chain [101181988] [SORT4CIRC] Page 6 on 42 Confidential 1 Excellence 1.1 Objectives and ambition 1.1.1 Main objective / overall purpose We generate 12.6 million tonnes of textile waste per year in the EU. Currently, only 22% of Post-Consumer Textile (PCT) waste is collected separately for reuse or recycling, while the remainder is often incinerated or landfilled. However, with the updated legislative directives on separate textile collection across the EU by 1 January 2025, the amount of textile waste collected will increase. To ensure the sustainable recovery of this material, upscaling sorting and recycling capacity and efficiency must go hand in hand. Sorting for reuse and recycling is the critical step of the value chain that decides the next best use and enables textile resources to cycle repeatedly through the supply chain (SC). Textile reuse clearly offers greater environmental and economic benefits than recycling, but its sorting is carried out manually and is associated with high labour and energy costs in the EU. Several brands and manufacturers are already incorporating recycled content in their collections; however, most are not using PCTs. To date, recycled fibre and fabrics made from PCTs are priced higher, intimately related to the high costs required to process them. Our overall ambition is to improve value creation and cost efficiency through automated sorting and innovative recycling. This will generate new revenue streams for sorters and recyclers by enhancing sorting for reuse and turning non-re-wearable textiles into valuable feedstock for high-value recycling. We will strive to unlock the current technological gaps for textile identification and value-chain traceability while providing an overall sound business case driving circularity within the Textile Value Chain (TVC). The use of modern digital technology in the sorting process will make it easier to achieve comprehensive information on the materials and fabrication process step. This information can then be integrated into automated Pre-Sorting and facilitating Fine Sorting (PSFS) and Pre-Sorting and Sorting for Recycling (PSSR) processes. The different complementary technologies are interlinked with interoperable architecture to achieve complete traceability. The results generated are cross-checked to ensure reliability and consistency, enabling informed decisions about the optimal channel for reuse or recycling besides ensuring high quality input to reuse and recycling. In response to this challenge, we will develop a systemic, transformative solution by pioneering scalable solutions for PSFS & PSSR, coupled with traceability and ‘Design for Disassembly’ (DfD). An innovative solution that we can develop and deploy across the industry actors, maximising the impact on waste reduction and resource conservation. Fostering a more resilient and sustainable textile sector, reducing the industry's reliance on natural resources and decreasing emissions, aligning with the objectives of the EU Green Deal Industrial Plan. The overall scope is described in Figure 1. 1 https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/%20en/ip_23_3635 2 Directive - 2018/851 - EN - EUR-Lex (europa.eu) 3 https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A52023DC0062 Figure 1 - Overall concept of SORT4CIRC [101181988] [SORT4CIRC] Page 7 on 42 Confidential We gather all relevant expertise to achieve the four strategic objectives in the following section: industrial collector and sorter, technology developers (machine learning (ML), Artificial Intelligence (AI), hyperspectral & spectroscopy solutions, Design-for-Disassembly (DfD) solutions, recyclers (chemical and mechanical), along with specialists in SC tracking and tracing, digital transformation tools, digital passports, blockchain technology and Internet of Things (IoT), textile Business Models (BMs), trade and Life Cycle Cost/Assessment (LCC/LCA). We will ensure the effective scaling and implementation of the various solutions at the demonstration and commercial levels. Pre-sorting classification (reuse or recycling):  We will develop a system based on computer vision categorisation that provides a smart and efficient automated solution to power the earlier stages of sorting, focused on sorting for reuse (waste hierarchy).  Around 15 categories are used in the sorting line as a pre-sorting step; the digital technology will automatically identify parameters such as the product category and condition (e.g., damp and soiled).  We will increase the productivity, profitability, and sustainability of textile waste management by automating PSFS and increasing the fixed throughput of sorted textiles.  We will develop a Stock Keeping Unit (SKU) retrieval software to visually identify PCTs using AI-based computer vision, allowing access to product data for those without a readable identifier.  We will set up generic/scalable/rapid/robust classification techniques using visible and NIR (Near InfraRed) imaging and suitable algorithms. Textile recognition in sorting for recycling:  We will leverage a novel comprehensive Multirange Spectroscopic Platform and hybrid ensemble modelling to efficiently provide an affordable and compact real-time monitoring system capable of tackling the complex monitoring requirements of the textile items.  We will develop a semi-industrial real-time recognition system to identify the features of multilayer materials in a single textile item (currently non-identifiable with the existing NIR spectroscopy system and, therefore, sent to waste).  We will develop a new, more efficient recognition system based on a set of complementary recognition techniques, which unlock the current limits of NIR identification and increases the reliability of measurement technologies for (a) identification of fibre material < 3%; (b) identification of textiles containing black carbon-based pigments; (c) Detection and identification of finishing agents and coatings; (d) PA6/PA66 and cotton/linen differentiation; (e) identification of chemical contaminants; (f) identification of multilayer materials.  We will integrate and combine different technologies into an efficient and replicable system, enabling the management of raw material flows that meet standardised requirements and sorting criteria for recycling technologies. DfD for recycling:  We will assess DfD solutions using a Digital Product Passport (DPP) for identification and innovative technology to identify and disassemble textile products using heat-dissolvable bio-based and synthetic stitching threads and thermal disassembly systems. Key Performance Indicators Pre-sorting classification (reuse or recycling):  We will create a standard of pre-sorting categories the sorting operators use representing >20% of the EU market.  Precision levels for accuracy of garment type recognition ≥ 90%, wear conditions (soiling) classification ≥ 75%.  By using the automated PSFS, it will lead to a reduction of costs for the sorting process step by 20-30%.  Detection and retrieval of product data within 800 milliseconds for SKU retrieval.  Enriched data repository including ≥20 tonnes of various garments as ML reference models. Textile recognition for recycling:  A new hyperspectral dataset of garments for new code developments.  We will decrease the composition estimation error to a value of less than 5%.  We will enable to estimate the presence of one material with less than 3% content.  We will allow the detection of black textiles (cont. carbon pigments), chemical contaminants, and foreign materials.  Fabric structure recognition accuracy >95%.  We will achieve efficient separation of PET (Polyethylene terephthalate) fibres from multilayer textiles with >80% of PET and no metallic parts. Multilayer disassembly for recycling:  We will increase from 40-60% to 90-95% of recovered textile products from the disassembly process.  At pre- and post-disassembly stage, eco-designed garments enable >95% of accurate identification/sorting.  We will successfully identify DfD smart stitch via DPP Radio Frequency IDentification (RFID) tags. Objective 1: Revolutionize textile sorting by employing innovative, intelligent, interconnected technologies within an automated system to ensure an efficient and optimal channel for reuse or recycling. 1.1.2 Specific objectives [101181988] [SORT4CIRC] Page 8 on 42 Confidential  We will develop a textile customised tokenised RFID (TTRFID) tag model for autonomous/interoperable data communication.  We will design a textile DPP via eco-friendly blockchain architecture for traceability of data over the TVC ecosystem.  We will integrate the DPP data retrieval with the automated PSFS & PSSR solutions in Objective

Table 3.1d: List of Milestones 
Table 3.1e: Critical Risks for Implementation  
Description of Risk WP Risk Mitigation Measures  
Task 1.1: Digestate was found to be toxic for 
microalgae. Likelihood: low, severity: medium 
1 After identification of toxic compounds, strategies to 
reduce toxicity will be used (e.g. fed-batch feeding). 
Task 1.4: Slow rate of CO2 delivery into microalgae 
culture by the membrane carbonation system. 
1 Operating pH of microalgae culture to be optimised to 
drive the forward equilibrium of CO2 dissolution. Light 
intensity to be optimised to maximise photosynthesis. 
Task 2.1: Algae create a fouling layer of “kamikaze” 
cells that block any harmful light. Likelihood: high, 
severity: high 
2 Effective probe design utilising multiple spectroscopic 
methods. 
Task 2.3: Plating medium favours another class of 
bacteria compared to liquid medium. Likelihood: 
medium, severity: medium. 
2 16S rDNA will also be conducted on the liquid media 
and alternative plating media will be proposed. 
Task 3.1: Different species exhibiting varying degree 
of cell-wall weakening during autolytic incubation. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: low.  
3 Conditions for autolytic incubation will be made more 
extreme to induce cell-wall weakening (e.g. longer 
incubation time, N2 sparging) 
Task 3.2: Stirring forms emulsions resulting in 
significant solvent loss (<95% solvent recovery 
target). Likelihood: low, severity: medium 
3 Milder mixing technologies and other techniques to 
minimize solvent trapping (e.g. pH adjustment) will be 
simultaneously investigated and combined.   
Task 4.1: Low yield of suitable hydrocarbon chains 
from HEFA-SPK reactions with algal lipids. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: medium  
4 Multiple strategies in hydrotreatment steps, using 
separate hydrodeoxygenation, isomerisation, cracking 
catalysts, and catalysts will be pursued. 
Task 4.2:  Unsuccessful integration of HEFA-SPK 
with H2 production. Likelihood: low, severity: 
medium. 
4 Separate the two subprocesses to produce SPK, propane 
and H2 independently; this will still allow the new route 
to be more self-sufficient. 
Task 4.3:  Algae TCR oil leads to low-quality fuel. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: medium 
4 A more drastic set of temperature and pressure to be 
applied during hydrotreatment. 
Task 4.4: Purification steps are too complex and 
costly.  Likelihood: low, severity: high. 
4 Change the hydrotreatment steps in Task 4.1 to minimise 
purification requirements. 
Task 5.2:  Lack of accurate available data for each 
stage of life cycle, particularly from real world SAF 
manufacturing facilities (due to limited access to 
commercial-scale plant or IP restrictions). 
Likelihood: medium, severity: low 
5 We are currently partnered with the leading global SAF 
development company SkyNRG conducting a feasibility 
study for commercial scale SAF manufacturing in Ireland, 
providing direct access to accurate data in SAF 
manufacturing. 
Task 5.2: Project carrying out activities which are not 
in-line with the RSB Certification. Likelihood: 
medium, severity: low.  
5 Task 5.2 will start in M1 so help align project activities 
with RSB EU RED Certification standards from project 
commencement.
7.5 RSB EU RED Fuel 
Certification 
Attain the RSBs EU RED Certification to achieve 
internationally recognised sustainability standards

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

GLOSSARY AI ELH ELT EOL EC EU FAIR GHG HMLS HSI IV ICP-MS Artificial Intelligence End-of-life hose End-of-life tyre End-of-life European Commission European Union Findable Accessible Interoperable Reusable Greenhouse gas High-Modulus Low-Shrinkage Hyperspectral imaging Intrinsic viscosity Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry LCA LCC LIBS Mt / kt / t NIRS PET POC REF SSP XRF r-PET v-PET 3T Life-Cycle Assessment Life-Cycle Cost Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy Million tonnes / kilo tonnes / tonne Near Infrared Spectroscopy Polyethylene terephthalate Proof of concept Reference Solid state polycondensation X-ray Fluorescence Recycled Polyethylene terephthalate Virgin polyethylene terephthalate Tension, Time and Temperature EXECUTIVE SUMMARY We use PET widely in plastics and textiles, leading to over 20 Mt/y of complex waste worldwide for which no closed-loop recycling is viable today. Most complex waste is landfilled or incinerated. There is an urgent need for us to develop a circular solution to convert complex PET wastes from plastics and textiles back into high-added value products. WHITECYCLE unites 3 brand-owners, 1 PET converter, 2 waste managers, 1 digital deeptech for smart sorting, the world-leading enzymatic recycling SME, 1 LCA company, 3 UNIs/RTOs, 1 cluster, and 1 management firm. We aim to demonstrate two new processes combining strong scientific and industrial know-how: (i) innovative identification, sorting, and separation technologies that will dramatically increase the PET content of complex waste streams to 80%, and (ii) a disruptive enzymatic recycling process that is expected to yield pure PET monomers sustainably even for impure waste streams. We will repolymerise and recycle the PET monomers obtained. Thus, we will use 2 t of waste to demonstrate 3 highly technical PET-containing pilot series: 100 tyres, 1,500 m of lay-flat hoses, and 400 m² of multicomponent fabric that will be coated and used to manufacture 4 lines of technical garments. We will provide process design kits, LCA, and production cost estimates to PET manufacturers and waste management companies for rapid deployment and assure social acceptance. First projections show that our recycled PET will be competitive with virgin PET. We will conduct a full circle loop from real complex waste feedstock to representative product of the 3 use-cases at TRL 5. Then, a strong upscale study will allow the process steps to reach TRL 6 to 8. By 2030, we will enable the annual recycling of more than 2 Mt of PET, which corresponds to the amount of additional recycled PET needed to meet the EU’s 2030 targets. This will reduce emissions by around 2.06 Mt CO2eq and avoid the landfilling of more than 1.8 Mt of PET. CL6 -2021 -CIRCBIO -01 -04 CONFIDENTIAL WHITECYCLE PART B 3 1 Excellence 1.1 Objectives and ambition 1.1.1 General presentation of WHITECYCLE The European Commission’s (EC) Circular Economy Action Plan adopted in March 2020 has identified plastics as among the 7 key product value chains as a priority, given their environmental impacts and circularity potentials. Indeed, as the consumption of plastics is expected to double in the coming 20 years, the EC has taken targeted measures to address the sustainability challenges posed by this ubiquitous material and will continue to promote a concerted approach to tackle plastics pollution at a global level. To increase uptake of recycled plastics and contribute to the more sustainable use of plastics, the EC has proposed mandatory requirements for recycled content and waste reduction measures for key products such as packaging, construction materials, and vehicles, also considering the activities of the Circular Plastics Alliance. The European strategy is ambitious, envisioning the redefinition of the plastic value chain to make it 100% circular by 2050. It requires ambitious industrial actions coordinated at the European level. That’s why we aim at the industrial-scale implementation of 3 very different circular value chains by 2030, within 4 years after our project. We particularly address the use case of polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), the third most used polymer in the world with more than 82 Mt produced in 2019 worldwide and used in various applications (Figure 1). Figure 1: PET amounts in the two targeted applications i.e., plastics and textile in 2019 If PET recycling processes are commonly implemented for easy-to-collect and pure mono-material products (7 Mt of recycled PET (r-PET) produced in 2017 worldwide), the situation is very different for multi-material products, and massive volumes of PET waste currently end up landfilled or incinerated without any recycling possibility. Indeed, there are no available solutions at the industrial scale for the recycling or upcycling of these complex wastes due to current technical limitations related to the sorting, separation, and purification processes of the retrieved mono-materials. This complex mixed waste stream includes multilayer packaging, multicomponent textiles, and composite plastics (i.e., tyres, hoses, etc.). In a recent study, Singh et al. estimated 44 Mt of PET is used today in such complex compositions that it cannot be recycled with classical technologies. In our project, with conservative figures, our first estimation of eligible waste feedstock exceeds 20 Mt per year (1.5 Mt end-of-life tyres (ELT) and end-of-life hoses (ELH), 13.2 Mt multicomponent clothing, and 3.5 Mt multilayer packaging, 2 Mt of other goods and industrial applications). Such large amounts of waste generate soil and water contamination that can adversely affect soil and water biodiversity, significant greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and many other negative impacts on human health. Rethinking the functioning of such a complex value chain requires efforts by all its key players (i.e., plastics and textiles producers, end-users, recyclers, retailers, consumers) to drive investment in the right direction. The objective of WHITECYCLE is to overcome the barrier posed by feedstock complexity via a set of versatile, disruptive, and fully scalable technological processes (mechanical, physico-chemical, and enzymatic) capable of infinitely retrieving and recycling PET, even from complex mixed waste to yield pure, industrially relevant monomer at any scale. We include a clear plan for the replication of the technologies to be implemented at the industrial scale (i.e., > TRL8) beyond our project, across a wide range of sectors implementing or manufacturing PET-containing products. Information recorded in detail and in standardized ways (using FAIR data principles) will enable interoperability among the components, transparency of the processes involved, and the reuse in similar contexts as well as new, innovative ones (data as an asset). The main outcomes of our project will be: • 3 industrially viable circular value chains from bin to highly technical products namely tyres, lay-flat hoses, and multicomponent textiles at TRL 6 or above for the different steps (recognition, sorting, enzymatic depolymerization, purification, monomer reuse). • Comprehensive LCA, LCC, social acceptability studies, and FAIR data for each of the 3 use-cases • Agreements for upscaling and implementation from 12 brand-owners, 3 PET manufacturers, and 3 waste managers/recycling equipment manufacturers 1 IHS Markit 2020 2 Singh et al., Techno-economic, life cycle, & socioeconomic impact analysis of enzymatic recycling of PET, Joule (2021), doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2021.06.015 PET WORLDWIDE 82 Mt, 2019 61% TEXTILESPLASTICS 39% 33 Mt IN CLOTHING 16 Mt IN OTHER APPLICATIONS carpets, non-woven textiles, geotextiles, etc. 23 Mt IN PACKAGING 10 Mt IN END-PRODUCTS 19.8 Mt 100% PET 13.2 Mt Multicomponent clothing 3.5 Mt multilayer packaging 6 Mt opaque bottles 13.5 Mt clear bottles 3 Mt tyres, hoses & rubber goods 7 Mt other goods - electronic, acoustic & insulation CL6 -2021 -CIRCBIO -01 -04 CONFIDENTIAL WHITECYCLE PART B 4 1.1.2 USE-CASE #1: PET IN TYRES AND REINFORCED RUBBER GOODS We must find solutions at the EU scale to produce sustainable tyres, since natural resources are decreasing, while waste is increasing. ELT are non-biodegradable wastes, generated in large amounts all over the world. Worldwide, 1.6 billion new passenger car tyres are sold each year representing 11 Mt of ELT. Tyres are the only parts of the car that touch the road. They must strike a balance between safety, traction, durability, energy efficiency, and manufacturing cost. Due to these competing needs, tyres are made of very complex composites that perform in harsh conditions. Figure 2 depicts the overall structure of a passenger car tyre. A tyre is made up of elastomers (natural & synthetic rubber), filler (carbon black & silica), reinforcement material (metals & PET textiles based on highly technical fibre), plasticizers, and other chemicals. Some fractions are currently very well retrieved such as rubber and metal, but the embedded textile part composed of the carcass plies (thin textile PET fibre cords bonded into the rubber) and the cap plies made of PET, polyamide, or hybrid polyamide/aramid is not
